title: Build a Compound Component Select
prompt:
Build a `Select` compound component with sub-components `Select.Trigger`, `Select.Options`, and `Select.Option`.

Requirements:
- `Select` maintains open/closed state and the selected value
- `Select.Trigger` renders a button that opens the options list, displaying the current value
- `Select.Options` renders a dropdown list, visible only when open
- `Select.Option` renders an individual option; clicking it selects it and closes the list
- Sub-components share state through an internal Context — no prop drilling
- Accept an `onChange` callback on the root `Select` component

Demo: a color picker built entirely from `<Select>`, `<Select.Trigger>`, `<Select.Options>`, `<Select.Option>`.
context:
The **compound component pattern** inverts control: instead of a monolithic `<Select options={[...]} />`, the consumer assembles the UI from discrete sub-components. Each sub-component is self-contained but shares state through a private Context.

The pattern has three parts:
1. The root component creates a Context and provides open/selected state.
2. Sub-components consume that context via a custom hook (`useSelectCtx`).
3. The custom hook throws if called outside the root — fail-fast, not silent.

This pattern gives consumers full control over markup and styling while keeping business logic encapsulated. It is used by Headless UI, Radix UI, and Reach UI. The cost is higher initial complexity; the payoff is composability without an escape hatch.

resources.title: Kent C. Dodds – Compound Components with Context
React – Passing Data Deeply with Context
